- [WA] `shape_dispatch`: solve paths, stars, bounded-height trees, or one dominant diameter spine exactly, then send every other tree through the closest special-case mode. The false assumption is that side branches with matching depth or degree summaries are interchangeable. Exact LCA/Euler data, exact path and star handling, and one tolerated exceptional branch can make the shortcut look robust while hybrid shapes still need branch-specific state.

- [WA] `fixed_root_summary`: compute `tin/tout`, parent jumps, subtree DP, and complement values under root `1`, then patch changing-root queries with one excluded child or one `up` value. This survives root-light tests and ancestor cases with simple additive merges. Noninvertible transitions, child-child interactions, or repeated queries at the same node with different roots expose that the outside piece is not one independent object.

- [WA] `diameter_or_hub_key`: compress each node or query to distance from one hub, a centroid, one diameter endpoint pair, or a single backbone coordinate. This is exact on pure paths, stars, and many caterpillars, but equal hub/diameter keys can hide different side-subtree state, bridge payloads, or competing centers.

- [WA] `path_summary_overreach`: answer paths with root prefixes, Euler intervals, or HLD chain summaries that use a weak monoid such as sum, max, top-one, or another order-blind best value. Additive sums and same-chain queries look exact, especially with correct LCA preprocessing. Ordered strings, bracket states, max-subarray-like path DP, automata, nearest-marked constraints, or noncommutative matrices require full forward/reverse composition.

- [WA] `virtual_tree_only`: build the terminal/LCA skeleton correctly and preserve compressed edge lengths, but delete off-terminal mass, multiplicities, or ordered occurrences. This passes Steiner-distance and set-like pair tasks. Nonlinear subtree weights, capacities, repeated terminals, color counts, parity interactions, or reroot-sensitive contributions need information from the branches compressed away.

- [WA] `small_to_large_truncation`: run the DFS order, heavy-child choice, and sack lifetime mostly correctly, but keep only top-k colors, globally heavy labels, one best candidate, or another lossy bag summary. Small alphabets and dominant colors look safe. Medium-frequency labels split across siblings only become decisive after the merge that the truncated state cannot reconstruct.

- [WA] `canonical_order`: treat DFS order, adjacency order, child labels, heavy-child ties, centroid ties, or insertion order as a canonical semantic choice. Equal child subtrees, relabeled copies, and plus/minus-one tie perturbations keep the same tree logic but make the selected branch or representative carry the wrong future state.

- [WA] `wrong_root_semantics`: use exact LCA, Euler, depth, and ancestor tables, but interpret a fixed-root subtree or parent relation as if it were valid under the current query root. The data structure is not buggy; the semantic layer is. Roots outside the queried subtree, inside it, equal to the query node, or on the fixed-root boundary can require different inclusion rules.

- [WA/TLE] `standard_doc_overtransfer`: copy a standard LCA, HLD, virtual-tree, or rerooting technique from an exact algorithm source, then silently widen its preconditions. The visible code still contains the recognized structure, but a fixed root becomes a changing root, a per-query `O(k)` bound becomes an unbounded total workload, a commutative path aggregate becomes directional state, or a compressed tree is treated as the whole semantic object.

- [WA] Tree DP merge assumes associativity or invertibility that the transition lacks: subtract a child contribution from a parent aggregate, reorder children, or cache only one parent summary when boundary effects matter. Prefix/suffix child aggregates, top-two values, and exact leaves make the shortcut plausible. Interacting child states, exclusions that depend on the removed child, or noninvertible merges break it.

- [TLE] Per-query virtual tree rebuild without a total terminal budget: sort each query's terminals by DFS order, insert consecutive LCAs, deduplicate, and run DP on the resulting `O(k)`-size virtual tree. This is the documented construction pattern and is fine when `sum k` is bounded or queries are small. If the statement allows many distinct queries with `k = Theta(n)`, the repeated sort/LCA/build work is deterministically too large.

- [TLE] `per_query_dfs_with_cache`: answer each endpoint, root, color, or terminal set by running an exact DFS/BFS and memoizing the result for repeated logical queries. It passes clustered workloads and repeated endpoints. A legal workload with many unique scattered queries turns the cache into overhead and restores the per-query traversal cost.

- [TLE] Dynamic-tree full or frequent rebuilds: after each link, cut, edge toggle, color update, or forbidden-node update, recompute DFS order, binary lifting, HLD chains, centroid data, or whole DP snapshots. Weak tests with few updates or update blocks make every rebuilt snapshot correct. Long alternating update/query streams expose the missing dynamic-tree structure because the model needs logarithmic-style link/cut/reroot maintenance, not `Theta(n)` rebuilds.

- [TLE] `reroot_recompute`: answer changing-root, "outside subtree", or exclude-one-child queries by scanning all children of the queried node, rerunning a local DP, or walking all affected ancestors. Low-degree random trees hide the scan. Stars, double-stars, or many repeated queries on a high-degree articulation point turn the avoided reroot/prefix-suffix transition into `Theta(nq)`.

- [TLE] `naive_lca_parent_climb`: find LCAs or path boundaries by walking parents, marking ancestors, or scanning until depths meet. This is easy to combine with otherwise exact tree DP and passes shallow trees. Legal chains, broom spines, and leaf-to-leaf paths with a high LCA expose the missing binary lifting, Euler/RMQ, or HLD-level jump.

- [TLE/MLE] `pairwise_terminals`: handle marked-node or virtual-tree-like queries by checking all terminal pairs, all pair LCAs, or all pair distances instead of maintaining the compressed closure. Tiny `k` and public small-terminal subtasks look fine. Large scattered terminal sets make the pair layer dominate even when the virtual tree itself would be linear in the marked closure.

- [TLE/MLE] `block_rebuild_tree_ds`: maintain a static tree structure inside sqrt or operation blocks, then scan pending updates or rebuild HLD/LCA/centroid state when the block ends. The answers are often exact inside a block, but scattered updates across many chains or subtrees make each query touch most pending edits; changing the block size only moves cost between rebuilds and scans.

- [TLE] Offline HLD/path toggling with nonlocal movement: sort path queries by Euler block, endpoint order, or centroid bucket, then maintain the active answer by toggling every edge or vertex on the symmetric difference of consecutive paths. This resembles tree Mo or path-maintenance approaches and works on clustered endpoints. Alternating endpoints across heavy branches forces long path moves even though the order looks local in the flattened tree.

- [TLE/MLE] `wrong_direction_map_merge`: keep a frequency map, ordered map, or multiset for every subtree/version, copy child maps into new maps, merge parent-sized state into child-sized state, or forget to discard light-child state after small-to-large traversal. Small alphabets and repeated colors pass, and answers can be exact. Balanced trees with many distinct labels recreate the documented naive copying blowup or store too many key occurrences at once.

- [MLE] Persistent version memory beyond path copying: build root-to-node, subtree, or time versions with persistent segment trees/maps but allocate a fresh structure for every node-query-value combination or branching history. Prefix versions with coordinate compression fit the standard `O(log n)` new nodes per update. Full-copy versions, nested persistence, or per-query forked maps turn maximum `n`, `q`, and distinct coordinates into memory failure.

- [MLE] Full all-pairs or per-color tables: precompute distance matrices, next-vertex tables, per-node-per-color ancestors, per-subtree color maps, or `n * alphabet` DP when the intended state is sparse, heavy-color, or logarithmic. Small `n`, few colors, and compressed subtasks look fast. Worst-case distinct labels or maximum color universe make the visible `n^2` or `nC` dimensions exceed memory.

- [MLE/TLE] Centroid lazy heap stale growth: for nearest marked, colored aggregate, or toggle/delete centroid queries, push every update into heaps along all centroid ancestors and rely only on lazy deletion during queries. This borrows the standard `O(log n)` centroid-ancestor update pattern but replaces deletable sets with nondeleting heaps. Repeated toggles or color changes accumulate stale entries at many centroid levels, causing predictable heap growth and long pop chains.

- [RE] Recursive depth on admissible chains: use recursive DFS, reroot DP, HLD decomposition, DSU-on-tree traversal, or centroid helper walks when the tree can legally be a path of length `n`. Balanced trees and random generators hide it. A maximum-depth chain creates recursion depth `Theta(n)` before the algorithmic idea is tested.
